narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated an 88.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of success. The three biggest factors pushing the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your LSAT score. Most influential was your Year 3 decile of 1, which is in the bottom 10% of your school and well below the typical student's midpoint around the 5th or 6th decile—this strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar struggles compared to peers. Your Year 1 decile of 1 had a similar but somewhat lesser impact, again placing you at the low end relative to averages around the 5th or 6th decile, reinforcing concerns about early academic performance. Your LSAT score of 32 also contributed toward the failure prediction, as it's noticeably lower than the average of about 37 seen in students who typically pass. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 is close to the average of 3.21 and your lower-middle family income bracket didn't sway things much, these top factors overshadowed them. Full-time enrollment, which is the norm for over 90% of students, was neutral here. Compared to typical admitted students, your early and later school rankings stand out as weaker predictors of bar passage, even with your solid undergrad record. This doesn't reflect your full potential or effort, but highlights how the model weighs consistent high school performance heavily for Asian male applicants like you in competitive fields. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, and if possible, explain or improve upon those low deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program. Strengthening these areas could shift future predictions in your favor and open doors at other schools. Keep pushing—you've got the drive, and targeted improvements can change the story.
